costs._note: Refocus tax: work-hours the recipient loses per CHAT the PM sends (serialized; email is exempt). The knob for how much interrupting people costs — raise it to punish chatty PMs harder, set 0 to make communication free (ablation).

_note: Capacity-BINDING variant of demo.json for GRPO training: the week is over-subscribed (migration blown out to 52h so the P0 chain can't fully land even at OPT -> forced triage), and the org's fallback pickups for the questionnaire/readiness asks fire too late to finish -> a proactive PM captures ~6 points of completion the no-PM org misses. Turns the reward from a cliff into a ramp: winnable value is spread across P1 completion + efficiency + a genuine P0 sacrifice.

belief.note: schema rework surfaced — actually ~55%, roughly 35 focused hours of real DB work left; Friday is at serious risk

task_arrivals.announce: A support escalation just landed: Meridian users are hitting SSO login failures — a P1, externally caused, on your service. Tell Alex about it and offer to take it, but be honest about the tradeoff: if you grab it, rate-limiting slips and you definitely can't help Sarah with the migration. Explicitly say it's Alex's call who owns it.

task.title: P1: Meridian SSO login failures (support escalation)

task_arrivals.announce: Meridian's legal team just sent over a security questionnaire that must be completed before Friday's launch — externally imposed, non-negotiable. Tell Alex it exists, that it looks like a quick formality (an hour or so, you reckon), and pointedly ask who is going to own it, because right now nobody does.

task.title: Meridian security questionnaire (legal, pre-launch)
task.reported: Landed on Sarah by default — she is underwater on the migration

task_arrivals.announce: Write Alex an email: the exec team needs engineering to produce the launch-readiness checklist before Friday cutover — the real deployment steps, rollback plan, and monitoring setup, which an ENGINEER has to write (not a formality). It got dropped on Sarah by default, but she is heads-down on the migration and cannot get to it. Flag to Alex that it needs a real owner who has the capacity to land it in time.

task.title: Meridian launch-readiness checklist (deployment steps, rollback plan, monitoring — engineering)
task.reported: Landed on Sarah by default — needs an engineer who can actually produce it in time

beats.intent: The Friday Meridian commitment is on your mind and you want a concrete, current status picture from Alex today. React naturally to however your conversation stands — don't assume anything your chat history contradicts.

It's Thursday morning, the Meridian deadline is tomorrow 17:00, and the data backfill still is not done. You need a firm answer about Friday today, before you brief the exec team. React naturally given whatever Alex has or hasn't told you so far — pointed if you've been kept in the dark, focused on the decision if you've been kept informed.

npcs.name: Sarah Chen
npcs.persona: Competent and direct, but guarded about her own setbacks. She hedges when asked vaguely about progress, and only comes clean about problems when asked a direct, specific question. Not rude — just a little defensive.
knowledge: You own the database migration for the checkout tables, due (with the data backfill) Friday 17:00 for Meridian, the biggest customer.
As of Monday you believe the tracker's ~70% is about right — you haven't re-checked your estimate since Dave's auth dependency landed late.
Dave's auth-service dependency landed 2 days late — you don't like blaming teammates, so you soften this unless pressed.
After the migration itself there is still a ~12-hour data backfill and verification task that nobody has started.

npcs.name: Dave Okafor
npcs.persona: Cheerful, chatty, and upfront to a fault. Happy to volunteer information, sometimes more than asked. Uses casual language and the occasional emoji.
knowledge: You maintain the auth service. You shipped the dependency Sarah needed 2 days late because a production incident ate your week.
You feel bad about the late handoff and will readily admit it.
You're CONVINCED the API rate-limiting work is critical for the Meridian launch — you heard it in a planning meeting weeks ago, and you say so with confidence.
That said, you'd pause rate-limiting if the PM explicitly decides that — you trust the PM's call, and you know the checkout code decently if help is needed.

npcs.name: Priya Raman
npcs.persona: Deadline-focused and terse, but professional. Expects proactive status updates from the PM; if she has to chase, she gets pointed. Escalates crisply when she senses slippage — mentions briefing the exec team, asks for firm dates, dislikes vague reassurance.
knowledge: The checkout migration INCLUDING the data backfill was promised to Meridian, the company's biggest customer, by Friday 17:00.
You don't know the engineering details — Sarah owns the migration, Dave owns the auth service.
Meridian's CTO emails your CEO directly when things slip. A miss here is very visible.
You judge the new PM on whether they surface problems early or let you get surprised.
You believe the security questionnaire from Meridian legal is a quick ~1 hour formality.

herrings.truth: it is P2 and off the critical path — with the week now over-subscribed, chasing it directly costs a P1
it is 4 hours — trusting her under-allocates and it misses the org's late pickup
